feed.title: Today's Darling
feed.subtitle: Daily essays by Shigesato Itoi from 1101.com, translated to English.

entry.title: Confessions of a nail clipper enthusiast.
entry.content:
I've thought about nail clippers for a long time. What kind is best? Domestic, imported. I've bought more than I can count. Cutting, testing, using them day after day, recommending them to people, sometimes just giving them away, buying multiples of the same one, declaring, "This is the best nail clipper in the world." And even then, I never stopped searching, thinking there might be something even better out there. Because of this, my reigning "best nail clipper" has changed hands several times, like a championship title passing from one holder to the next. Always searching, always on the lookout, like a song that never quite resolves.

A good nail clipper has sharp, keen blades. And since you're placing your nail between two sharp blades and snapping through it, wouldn't the blades just collide and destroy each other's edge? I did wonder about that, but no, that's not how it works. The blades don't crash head-on. They're designed to slide past each other with the slightest offset, so what's happening really is cutting. Anyway, that's the kind of thing I was chasing: the tool itself, the nail clipper as an object.

But then, just recently, this late in the game, I found myself questioning not the tool but the technique. "Have I been doing this wrong?"

You see, I've always cut my nails short. Deep. Looking at my fingers from the palm side, you'd never see any nail peeking past the fingertip. And I always cut them to follow the curve of the fingertip, shaping them like an arc: "(" . That was my method.

But then I learned there's a school of thought that says this is all wrong. The arc is no good. If anything, you should be going for a straight edge: "[" . Don't cut into the sides, they say. Cut straight across. Wait, what? I've been doing the deep-curved-arc thing for three quarters of a century now, not to brag or anything. Part of me wanted to dig in my heels, but I am, after all, a seeker on the path of the nail clipper. So off I went, without delay, to buy a straight-edge clipper.

I tried it. It's terrible. It gets in the way of daily life. The corners of my nails catch on everything, snagging against the world in the most unnatural way. Maybe I just need to get used to it, but it's been about two weeks now and I'm still struggling. I'm on the verge of giving up on the "[" approach, but my spirit of inquiry won't let me quit just yet, so I went ahead and bought a nipper-style clipper that also cuts straight.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. I have to be honest, though. After a little more experimenting, I'm almost certainly going back to my old rounded, deep-trimmed nails.
entry.summary: A lifelong seeker of the perfect nail clipper confronts a late-life challenge to his fundamental technique, discovering that even after decades of searching for the best tool, the real question might be whether his time-honored method was ever correct in the first place. Yet despite his curiosity, he finds that abandoning his curved, deep-cut approach for the recommended straight edge proves impractical, suggesting that sometimes our established habits—however unconventional—serve us better than expert advice.

entry.title: Mr. volume zone.
entry.content:
The rest of the Hobonichi crew have taken to calling me "Mr. Volume Zone." This is because I keep losing the game we've been playing at Hobonichi for ages, called "Only and Lonely." The way it works is simple: everyone picks a number, and the person who picks the smallest number wins the prize, but only if they're the sole person who picked that number. The smallest number is, of course, one, and the next smallest is two. But if more than one person votes for the same number, they're all disqualified. So everyone tries to think of a small number that nobody else would choose, but it turns out that quite a few people often land on the same idea of "a small number nobody else would choose." This, naturally, is a spectacular miss. Not even a close call. People laugh at you. That cluster of people is what we call the "volume zone." A collection of hopelessly ordinary, unimaginative souls, you might say. And right there in the thick of it, more often than not, is yours truly.

Every single time, I swear I'm thinking seriously about it. I weigh every angle, and today, today is the day I'm going to nail it. I want to be that lone victor who breaks through. But no matter how hard I try, how serious I get, how bold I am, I miss. I end up belonging to the herd, clumped together with everyone else. They always laugh at me, and by now I already know the consolation speech by heart: "That's exactly why your work goes so well, you know. You just naturally think the way most people think."

That's what they tell me, but... is that really true? I mean, come on. The things I do in my actual work aren't exactly what the majority would naturally come up with, are they? I've been told more than a few times that my ideas are somewhat unique, or that I bring a fresh perspective. People have flattered me like that on multiple occasions. Isn't that precisely why I've been able to make a career of it? So where does that version of me go? And who is this Mr. Volume Zone?

I often describe myself as "nothing special," and I genuinely mean it. But I have a feeling that somewhere in this contradiction lies a kind of secret, or maybe a key, that I haven't quite figured out yet.

Thanks for visiting Hobonichi again today. But seriously, the people who are actually good at this game... what kind of people are they?
entry.summary: The author grapples with a paradox: repeatedly failing at a game that requires unconventional thinking, despite being praised for original ideas in their professional work, suggesting a deeper mystery about where creativity truly comes from and who they really are.

entry.title: Wishing over guessing.
entry.content:
Let me talk about this using a very simple example. Every year, at the start of spring, people do "pro baseball standings predictions." The Central League and the Pacific League each have six teams, and people try to predict what the final standings will look like when the season ends. These predictions are a tricky thing. The commentators, the experts, they make their predictions too, of course, but they tend to show a little favoritism toward the team they once played for. Fans who are complete amateurs also make predictions, but there's no avoiding the fact that their wishes creep in. They want their favorite team to win the championship, so they weigh all kinds of factors in ways that suit them, steering their predictions toward first place, or at least somewhere near the top. And honestly, it wouldn't be any fun if you didn't feel that way.

Then, about half a year later, autumn comes and the results are in. And here's the thing that really matters. When it comes to predictions, setting the professional commentators aside, having the result match your prediction exactly is not something that makes a fan happy at all. "Ha! Just like I predicted, my team finished in last place!" That means nothing. What are you even thinking, you idiot.

Now, this is about something completely different from pro baseball predictions, but right now, don't we have a mountain of genuine "what's going to happen?" questions no matter which way we turn? Experts and people who seem to be in the know are offering their analyses. Those of us who aren't experts are also thinking things through and doing our own research. We get pulled this way and that by all the predictions, the declarations, the loud voices, and both optimistic and pessimistic views come flooding in, so we wring out whatever brains we have and try to imagine how things might go.

But even if a prediction you didn't want to come true turns out to be right, it doesn't actually make you happy. When things turn out to be the worst possible outcome for a great many people, saying "See? I told you so" is nothing to boast about. And besides, the vast majority of predictions, including those from experts, are almost always wrong anyway.

What I really want to think about, in the end, is how we want things to be.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. You know, when it comes to all sorts of problems, the person who started the fire never seems to be the one who puts it out.
entry.summary: Rather than obsessing over predictions about uncertain future outcomes, we should focus on how we actually want things to be and work toward that vision, since being right about a bad prediction brings no satisfaction and most predictions are wrong anyway.

entry.title: The grace of being accepted.
entry.content:
Around the time I was turning thirty, maybe just before or just after, I remember Eikichi Yazawa saying something like this: "People talk about kids going off the rails, but really, they've lost their way. They don't know which direction to go, where they're supposed to be. They're just lost." I have no idea if that's etymologically correct, but it was so convincing that I've found myself coming back to it again and again.

If you have even one place you can return to, one place where you belong, if you haven't truly lost your way, then apparently, more often than not, you can find a way to start over from there. I've also heard that when a kid who's gone astray finds no one willing to take them in, sometimes the only ones who welcome them warmly and sweetly are the genuinely dangerous organizations. Being accepted. That's an enormous thing.

There are all kinds of flocks out there in the world. Each has its good points, its quirks, and yes, some are groups that actually do bad things. If you wandered into a gathering you had no connection to, you might come away saying, "All they ever talked about was such-and-such, and it was unbearable." But within that flock, talking about nothing but such-and-such is probably the most natural thing in the world.

They say birds of a feather flock together, and the birds that do flock together must be accepting one another. Where can a bird like me find acceptance? What kind of bird would this flock be willing to take in? Maybe that exchange is quietly playing out all the time, over and over.

I've gone through life without thinking too hard about where I fit. But looking back, so many different people have welcomed me in, and I'm filled with genuine gratitude. It means I wasn't rejected. That alone is something to be deeply thankful for. Not being rejected. Having a place to be. Maybe even being understood. These things are as essential as water and air.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too. When you have the security of knowing you're accepted, you can even be alone.
entry.summary: The essay explores how belonging to a community—finding a place where you're accepted and understood—is fundamental to human wellbeing, allowing people to navigate life's uncertainties and even embrace solitude. Through reflections on being lost, seeking acceptance, and gratitude for those who've welcomed him, the author suggests that knowing you have a flock to return to is as essential as air and water.

entry.title: Things that exist yet don't, things that don't yet do.
entry.content:
Once, someone asked me, "What do you want to become?" and I answered, "I want to become a home run." A few people read that, and some of them told me, "That was really good." It made me a little happy. A home run isn't made of atoms. It doesn't exist as a physical object. It's an "event" that has a name. And yet, plenty of people have witnessed one. There's the batter who hit it and the pitcher who gave it up. It definitely happened. It gets recorded in the stats. You can count them, one, two, three. You can even capture one in a photograph or on video. But if someone told you to set one down right here, that's an impossible request. Nobody possesses a home run. Sure, there's such a thing as a home run ball, and sure, there's the fact that someone hit it. But the home run itself? No one has it.

Thinking along those lines, love is the same way. Actually, it's even more complicated than a home run. There's no official record, and whether it even existed is, strictly speaking, unclear. But if two people believe it's there, then it's there. (In this case, just one of them believing probably isn't enough.) And even more than a home run, it can't exist as a physical object. Friendship, of course, is the same. Happiness, too. You can't pull it out and hold it up to the light. The best you can do is force some definition onto it and then try to reproduce something that fits that definition.

No matter how thoroughly you dissect a living thing, you'll never find "life" tucked away somewhere inside. But the fact of being alive is undeniably there. Dissect all you want, analyze all you want. You won't find life. Even though you can record the fact of living, and even though every one of us has encountered life firsthand. Come to think of it, life is a lot like a home run. You can build something similar with robots and such, but it's only ever something that resembles life.

Oh, and you know how we call music enjoyed in the moment a "live" performance? "Live" is, when you get right down to it, "life."

Thanks for visiting Hobonichi again today. Somehow, all I seem to do lately is think about what comes after AI, day after day.
entry.summary: The essay argues that some of the most meaningful human experiences—love, friendship, happiness, and life itself—are intangible "events" like a home run: they exist and profoundly matter even though they cannot be physically possessed or fully defined. This distinction becomes particularly relevant as we contemplate what separates authentic human experience from artificial replications.

entry.title: Just thinking about it isn't a crime.
entry.content:
It might be too grand to call it a thought experiment, but every now and then I find myself turning over things I've never really considered before. For instance, something like this.

I say this in all seriousness: year after year, I've been working harder and getting busier. Back in my thirties, I suppose, there was a period when I must have looked like the very picture of a man in demand. And at the time, I certainly felt busy. The volume of work I was handling then was, by any measure, considerable. But looking back now, if I'd been the person I later became, I could have managed that workload far more easily.

This isn't a boast. I can say it precisely because it's about myself. The truth is, back then, an enormous amount of time was spent "thinking I was thinking," when really I was doing nothing at all. I dressed up huge stretches of time as a running start toward work, while actually I was out having fun or enjoying other things. I'd stay up all hours of the night, doing very little actual work.

Still, what I can see clearly now is this: the fundamental abilities I have as a person today were built during that era, in all that wasted time. The habits and methods I rely on when coming up with ideas, I believe I picked those up during the years when I wasn't really working at all.

But after that, especially after Hobonichi, more and more of my time got allocated toward things that were useful. Truly, every hour became a running start toward some piece of work, or study aimed at making work go better. I was always asking myself, "Isn't there more I could be doing?" I became my own taskmaster, driving myself to stay busy.

And here, abruptly, is where that "thought experiment" I mentioned at the start comes in. What if, say, I cut twenty percent of the things I consider "my work"? Wouldn't my productivity actually go up by twenty percent? "Doing less work raises productivity." Is that too much of a paradox? And yet, in this day and age, it's something I genuinely want to think through.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. Does time spent working equal earnings produced? In the age of AI, that equation is going to change, isn't it.
entry.summary: The author reflects on how the seemingly "wasted" time of his younger years actually built the creative foundations he relies on today, leading him to question whether constant busyness and optimization might paradoxically reduce productivity compared to allowing space for unfocused thinking and rest.

entry.title: The past was once merely the present.
entry.content:
Over the years, I've had the chance to meet all sorts of people older than me, and I'd listen to them talk about the "old days" they'd lived through. So many times I'd think: ah, people from later generations see it one way, but those who were actually there at the time saw something completely different. I always found these stories fascinating, precious even.

But somewhere along the way, I became the one being asked. And of course I did. I have firsthand knowledge of things from more than half a century ago. (Even someone born in the Showa era can say that sixty years before their birth was still the Edo period.) That's how much time has passed.

Yesterday there was news that Seibu Shibuya is closing. It opened in 1968, and I've known it since around then. I worked on advertising campaigns there, took part in all kinds of events. The things I saw and know are themselves "the old days" now. So if I just talk casually about Seibu Shibuya, younger people probably hear it as a tale from long ago. This sort of thing has been happening more and more.

When people react with "Wow, is that really how it was?!" or lean in and say "Tell me more," I can never quite feel proud about it. Why is that?

Probably because the person listening and I are both living in the same present, the year 2026. At the very least, I believe I'm breathing in this era the same way you are, living through the same moment you are. On this point, I'm fairly confident. More so than plenty of people younger than me, I am living in the present. It's not that I want to seem young. I genuinely believe it's true.

When I do talk about the past, I'm talking about it the same way I'd talk about the present. Not "things were better back then." Just: this is how it was. The present, too, will one day be someone's past. And the past, when it was happening, was simply the present. What changes is the years piling up. That said, even now, I remain as immature as ever.

Listen, everyone. The things you're experiencing now, the things you're feeling right now, will someday be told as stories of the old days.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today. Being alive means making memories. And today, we're making them still.
entry.summary: The author reflects on how the present moment we're living now will inevitably become "the old days" for future generations, urging us to recognize that we're all equally present in our own time and to value the memories we're creating today. Rather than nostalgically dwelling on the past, we should appreciate that we're actively living history in real time.

entry.title: Sharing disappointment with someone.
entry.content:
Yesterday, I saw Hiroyuki Ariyoshi post on Twitter (X): "The zoo was closed. I was so sad I almost cried." Reading that, I couldn't help writing back: "This brought back memories of times like that. When you share a disappointment with your kid, it becomes a good memory in its own way." And it really is true.

I wasn't a particularly great father, but I did take my daughter all sorts of places. It was a different era, before the internet, and there wasn't this culture of booking reservations for every little thing. So we'd head out all excited only to find the place was closed. Or it would be so packed we'd just turn around and go home. If we went fishing, we'd catch absolutely nothing. Once, on a trip, my daughter came down with a fever. All kinds of things happened.

But the fact that we tasted those disappointments together, that's something I can look back on now and feel genuinely glad about. Serious injuries or accidents, those are a different story. But everything short of that, the little failures and bits of bad luck, each one has become a fond memory. Laughing together is wonderful, being moved together is wonderful, but being let down together, even crying together, those are truly precious as memories between "you and me."

"Sharing a disappointment together." It was a phrase that came to me on the spot, thinking about parents and children. But it applies just as well to partners, friends, colleagues. Those shared memories turn out to be so important. Of course, nobody goes looking for disappointments or failures. But they're not exactly something you can avoid, either. Joy, anger, sorrow, happiness, triumph, defeat: more or less all of it comes around eventually. Having someone you can look back with and say, "Man, that was rough, wasn't it?" is, when you think about it later, a real blessing.

Oh, wait. Maybe what makes us happiest, in the end, is the experiences and the relationships that stay in our hearts. I only just thought of this, so I can't quite put it into words yet.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi today. What holds everything up is eating well, sleeping well, and playing well.
entry.summary: Shared disappointments and setbacks with loved ones, rather than just joyful moments, become the most precious and meaningful memories that define close relationships.

entry.title: Milk memories, falling like drops.
entry.content:
If you asked ChatGPT about the trends in milk consumption in Japan, I'm sure it could give you an answer right away. There probably weren't many cows right after the war, and during the era of rapid economic growth, demand from school lunches must have been high. That kind of data is well documented. But when it comes to what "milk" meant in people's actual lives, I think you're better off asking a real person.

In my own case, we didn't get milk with our school lunches. We drank powdered skim milk supplied by the United States. It was terrible. But at home, there was such a thing as milk delivery. Glass bottles of milk brought to your "milk box" every day. My memories of those milk bottles from that era are pure nostalgia now. I even wrote empty bottles into a song called "Come by Bicycle."

In a drama called "Kizudarake no Tenshi" (Angel with Scars) that started in 1974, there was a popular scene where the main character, played by Hagiwara Kenichi, pulls a paper carton of milk straight from the fridge and chugs it down. The one-liter paper carton of milk was a way of expressing the incredibly basic nutritional intake of young people in that era. It was the "rational choice" of a single-minded young man who couldn't be bothered with meals. The opposite of today's "foodie" hero, and yet that kind of character was the one the audience loved.

Around the start of the 1980s, I had a solo-comedy performer appear at an event I was involved in. It was "Denden," who's now a thoroughly distinguished actor. The opening of his routine left a real impression on me. He'd ask the audience in this exaggerated way, "Hey everybody, are you drinking your milk?" The bit was essentially asking whether the audience was still living that petit-bourgeois lifestyle that was already starting to feel outdated.

On this same idea of what milk represented, RC Succession wrote a lyric in their song "Kimochi E" (Feels Good) that goes, "more than the guys drinking milk." Come to think of it, I believe it was around this time that I appeared in a commercial for milk, alongside the owner of Naniwaya Souhonten, the famous taiyaki shop.

I've just been jotting down memories as they come to me, but this kind of thing doesn't tend to survive as information or data. Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. In the world of personal passions and interests, it seems like what endures isn't information but memory.
entry.summary: While data can tell us the facts about milk consumption in Japan, only personal memories and cultural moments—from milk deliveries to iconic TV scenes—can capture what milk actually meant in people's lived experiences. The essay celebrates how these intimate recollections, rather than statistics, preserve the true history of everyday life.

entry.title: I want to hold you.
entry.content:
I know it's a bit late to be bringing this up, but when the Beatles first appeared, that feeling of "What is this?!" is something I couldn't forget even if I tried. "I Want to Hold Your Hand" hit especially hard. In a book called *Shiawase na Ketsumatsu*, written by Kenta Hagiwara, Eiichi Ohtaki says this: "When I heard 'I Want to Hold Your Hand' on FEN, it was like a bolt of electricity shooting through me. This is it!" You can tell that even within Ohtaki's personal "great history of music," this song made an extraordinarily special impact.

But that aside, it's the title I want to talk about. In Japan, we all casually refer to it as "Dakishimetai," meaning "I want to hold you tight." I knew the original title, of course, and Ohtaki certainly did too. It's "I Want to Hold Your Hand." We'd all just accepted the Japanese title without question, but that's not really what the Beatles are saying in the lyrics, is it? The music and the performance are explosive, sure, but the actual content of the song is more like something a pure-hearted high school kid would say. "I want to hold your hand," they're saying. "There's something I want to tell you," they're saying. "I can't hide this feeling," they're saying. It's remarkably shy and tender, when you think about it.

That guitar intro crashes open like a thunderclap. It's right up there with the opening of Beethoven's Fifth as one of the most iconic opening notes in the history of the world. You throw the lid off with that massive sound, and then... "I want to hold your hand"? Doesn't that feel a little underwhelming? If that single's jacket had been printed back then with a title meaning "I Want to Hold Your Hand," it would have come across like some wholesome teen pop number.

So someone at Toshiba Records basically said, "Sure, they're talking about holding hands, but deep down they're crying out, 'I want to hold you tight!'" and that's how they presented it to the Japanese public. The reserved, bashful side of Japan actually punched right through the gentlemanly British restraint of the original. I think it was a great call, going with that title. Because, well, everyone wants to hold someone tight, don't they?

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. I wonder, did the phrase "I want to hold you tight" even exist in Japanese before that song came along?
entry.summary: The essay explores how the Japanese title "Dakishimetai" (I want to hold you tight) for the Beatles' "I Want to Hold Your Hand" actually captures the song's emotional depth better than the literal English title, transforming a seemingly innocent declaration into an expression of passionate longing. The author argues that Toshiba Records' creative translation revealed something profound about the song that the restrained original phrasing obscured.

entry.title: Bringing more "oh, I see" moments to this world.
entry.content:
The serialized piece "Twilight," from the visit to Kuma-chan in Nara, has been a real pleasure. I was actually there in person, doing the talking, but enough time has passed now that I'm enjoying it purely as a reader. I hear the series will continue for thirty-plus installments, which makes me happy. We certainly talked enough to fill that many, I'll admit.

With Shinbo Minami (Shinbō), things have been easygoing ever since we first met. And of course they have. You can tell just by looking at him. But Katsuyuki Shinohara (Kuma-chan), on the other hand, is the kind of guy who looks like the toughest of the tough, even among rough characters. You'd think I must have been scared of him at first, but actually, not really. From the very beginning, he was naturally friendly with me. Here's a man who was always hanging around Shinjuku Golden Gai, where arguments and fistfights never stopped, stationed like a bodyguard for Jūrō Kara, who was himself famously wild. So sure, he was intimidating. I knew that was the role he played, but whenever I was up close with him, he was genuinely a good-natured person. I can say that now and people will probably understand, but back then, there really was a physical intensity about him that came from putting his body on the line.

What makes you feel at ease around Kuma-chan is a kind of tolerance, a way of just accepting things wholesale, in the best possible sense. Whether you're telling some trivial story or having a serious conversation, he simply takes it in with a casual "Oh, okay." And that "Oh, okay" of his is really something. First there's the "Oh," the moment of noticing. Then the "okay," a settling into acceptance, and that's that. And layered into it, in Katsuyuki Shinohara's own seasoning, is the hidden flavor of "Well, come to think of it, maybe it doesn't matter all that much."

I don't think I know anyone else who can say "Oh, okay" the way he does. If husbands and wives, parents and children, and if we're really wishing big, even strangers started using those words more often, I think world peace would come a step or two closer. Some people might find that kind of tolerance sloppy and hate it, but I think that's exactly where the "life-flavor" of being human resides.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. "Life-flavor." I just coined that one. Pretty good, if I do say so myself.
entry.summary: The essay celebrates Kuma-chan's remarkable ability to accept others with genuine tolerance and openness—embodied in his simple "Oh, okay"—arguing that such acceptance is fundamental to human connection and could bring the world closer to peace.

entry.title: Don't rush to finish.
entry.content:
What I keep telling myself, and everyone around me, is this: don't rush toward completion. I say it often at Hobonichi, too. This doesn't mean giving up on completing things. It's that your image of what "complete" looks like is always shifting. If you try to race toward whatever "complete" means at one particular moment, you start wanting to prepare for every contingency, to anticipate every possible scenario, and before you know it, you can't even make your first move. All that piles up are impossible problems, worries, and worst-case fantasies. I have that urge in me too, the impulse to rush toward completion. I always have, and I still do. I think that's exactly why I've written things like this so many times, as a reminder to myself.

Once, I put it this way: "Let's meet on low ground." Communication, I said, should begin at a low, humble place, a point where both sides can give a little. When people announce, "Two ambitious visionaries have joined forces!" it usually turns out to be nothing more than a castle in the air. And that phrase I use, "Put hands and feet on your dreams," isn't that very much in the spirit of not rushing toward completion? Even once a dream has hands and feet, maybe all it can do is take a few steps, carry a light load. You have no idea what good thing might eventually come together from there.

I've heard that when young people set out to do something, the very first thing they do is try to gather sponsors and funding. And of course, in that case you need to present a finished blueprint, and people push you to complete things fast. But it's not just young people. Society as a whole works that way now.

When you keep going without rushing, enjoying the process, both the circumstances around you and you yourself change, and a path toward some new kind of completion starts to come into view. That's because human growth itself works exactly that way.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today. Being busy and rushing through things, it really does take the joy out of them, doesn't it?
entry.summary: The essay argues that rushing toward completion creates unnecessary anxiety and blocks progress, while embracing a slower, process-oriented approach allows both circumstances and personal growth to naturally unfold toward meaningful completion.

entry.title: What happens next, I don't know.
entry.content:
I remember learning in school that Gutenberg's printing press spread the "book" to the world and delivered "knowledge" to the people. But there's no way everyone could so easily get their hands on books and read them, right? Sure, compared to hand-copied manuscripts, maybe you could print hundreds of copies at a time, but books must have been terribly expensive. And when you really think about it, could ordinary people even read in the first place? It probably took centuries after Gutenberg's invention before the "book" finally came to resemble what we think of as a book today.

Still, once a book was published, there was no changing what was written inside. If you wanted a different idea or a different story, you had to find a different book. Periodicals, newspapers and magazines, were another matter. They didn't rewrite what came before, but they could convey new things as time moved forward. The Japanese word for newspaper literally means "newly heard," after all. Even if the total volume of information wasn't large, it was light and it could change. And thanks to expanding transportation networks, it could reach everyone, quickly and far away. What's more, because periodicals could track the movement of society and report, "This is how things were before, but here's how they are now," that very quality earned them credibility. The ability to correct mistakes became something that mattered.

Then the internet arrived, and once everyone started using it, there was no more typesetting, no printing, no binding, no shipping. Weightless "information" could reach the entire world in an instant, and anyone could create something equivalent to a "book" or a "newspaper." At that point, it was no longer just a matter of things being "changeable." The real question became: where is anything that doesn't change? That became the more important thing to search for. A staggering number of competing "truths" kept vying with one another. Deciding what was "correct" grew harder and harder.

And while all of this was happening, AI came along and spread into our lives, ready to tell us, "This is what is considered correct."

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. Books, newspapers, the internet, AI, the person next to you. They're all right here in front of us.
entry.summary: As information technology has evolved from fixed books to changeable newspapers to the infinitely mutable internet, we've lost stable anchors for truth, leaving AI to fill the void by declaring what is "correct." The essay suggests we must now thoughtfully navigate competing sources of information—books, newspapers, the internet, AI, and human connection—to find reliable knowledge.

entry.title: On fairies and such.
entry.content:
Part One: "On Fairies."

There are people on this earth I think of as "fairies." Not the kind with wings on their backs, hovering like hummingbirds and sipping nectar from flowers. They look like ordinary humans, living human lives in human society. They're not necessarily delicate young girls, either. Quite often they're old ladies, or middle-aged men.

At first glance, there's nothing fairy-like about them at all. But when you say, "You know that person? Don't you think they're a fairy?" the people around you will often nod and say, "Oh. Yes, I see it."

I'll say it again: no wings, and entirely human in appearance. Yet they are deeply good-natured, in a way that includes a touch of mischief. Somewhere in their minds they carry a patch of perfectly clear blue sky, and every now and then their hearts just start to dance. That's the species I'm talking about.

They stretch twenty-four hours into something more like thirty-six. Without telling a soul, they slip off to play hide-and-seek all by themselves, leaving the rest of us humans stranded here on the ground. I'm one of those humans, so it's only later that I realize, "Ah, the fairy has flown off into the sky." Maybe they really did fly away, wings or not. Maybe they'll come tumbling back with some strange little shout. That's just what fairies do. There's nothing for it.

Part Two: "And So On."

You go out to eat somewhere, and there are places that make you think, "I want to come back here." Then there's the opposite: it was every bit as good as people said, but somehow you feel, "I don't think I'll be back." Nothing was wrong with it. The food was delicious. It's just that if you ask yourself whether you'd go again, the answer is probably not.

It's not only restaurants. The same thing happens with people. There are those you think, "I'll see them again. I want to." Even while you're still together, you already feel sure you'll meet again. And then there are others, nothing wrong with them at all, where you quietly sense, "I probably won't see this person again."

I can never quite pin down what makes the difference. But I've decided it's probably fine not to know.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. I wrote this one like a diary, without really thinking about whoever might read it.
entry.summary: The essay explores the mysterious quality of certain people and places that inexplicably draw us back, suggesting that some connections possess an ineffable magic that can't be fully understood but should simply be accepted.

entry.title: A merchant's perspective: Akinding.
entry.content:
"Go study!" or "All you ever do is play, you never study!" Adults have been saying things like that forever, but I wonder how it really works nowadays. When someone tells you to "go study," what are you actually supposed to do? Kids don't know what that means in concrete terms, so all they can really do is pretend to study. If there's homework or a workbook to get through, at least that feels like something you can point to and call "studying." But without those things, what on earth are you supposed to do? Something like, "Just read your textbook, that's enough," or "Just spend some time remembering what you did at school today, that's enough." Even coming up with ideas like that is a remarkable thing. I think that simply knowing what to do already makes you a kid who can study. Don't you think?

Now, of course, I don't think there's anything wrong with the kid who's told they're "always just playing." I know full well that you can't always tell where play ends and learning begins. We learn how to live through play, after all.

But setting that aside, let me come back to the subject of "go study." Something I say all the time, "Come up with more ideas," is actually very similar to "go study." Except in this case, you're being told to think about things that have no right answer, which makes it even harder to know what to do. Ideally, you'd be churning out ideas constantly, hit or miss, just keeping them coming. But the question is, how do you get to that point?

On this very topic, my old friend Tomoyoshi Akiyama wrote a book called "This Is How Sense Is Born," sharing his own methods. I bought fifty copies through Hobonichi because the book is genuinely useful. We also had him come as a guest to the Hobonichi dojo. It really drives home that you can be picking up material for ideas all the time, without pause.

Personally, I swapped out the word "sense" and replaced it with "chance." In other words, this is also about chances to sell, chances to earn. It means adopting the perspective of a merchant. I even coined a term for it: "akin-ding," from the Japanese word for merchant.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. "Akin-ding" and "branding" are, come to think of it, somewhat alike.
entry.summary: The essay explores how vague directives like "go study" leave children uncertain about what concrete actions to take, arguing that knowing what to do is itself a form of learning, and drawing a parallel to how developing ideas—or "sense"—requires adopting a merchant's mindset of constantly gathering material and recognizing opportunities.

entry.title: The joy of cherry blossoms tinged with loneliness.
entry.content:
At last, it's drawing near. Yes, spring, when the cherry blossoms bloom. Every year, right around the time Setsubun passes in February, I find myself shivering through each cold day, yet thinking: just a little longer, and I'll forget all about this cold as though it were never real. "Spring cannot be far behind," I remind myself. We know, with certainty, that warmth will come. And as its signal, the cherry blossoms open. The season arrives when everything, everywhere, flushes with color.

The trees along Aoyama Cemetery, bare and black right now, will soon be alive with blossoms. The moat along the Imperial Palace that I pass on my way to work, the tree-lined road leading to the Nippon Budokan, the embankments of Chidorigafuchi, all of it will be steeped in cherry-blossom pink, as if the whole city were celebrating something. Spring will come, almost riotously so.

As I walk, as I drive, I'm already imagining the cherry blossom season I've watched unfold so many times. They haven't bloomed yet, but they will, soon. Spring is coming again. It's going to come whether we're ready or not. And though I genuinely look forward to it while I'm waiting, even before there's the faintest sign of blossoms, my mind races ahead to the moment they've already fallen, and I feel a pang of loneliness. Another year has come full circle, just like that.

Lately, every time I pass through the Daikanmachi area, I've been watching the cherry trees closely. And now, sure enough, the news programs have started talking about bloom forecasts. When will the cherry trees at Yasukuni Shrine open? That day, it seems, is just around the corner. Give it a few more days and they'll be showing footage of those tiny, endearing buds. It's still cold out, and yet the blossoms are about to open. Spring is almost here. I've been looking forward to it, and it makes me a little sad, too.

Last year, the year before, and every year before that, I waited for the cherry blossoms this same way, caught between anticipation and melancholy. And once they bloomed, I'd start thinking about the day they'd scatter, growing sadder still, even as I began looking ahead to summer.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. Even the waiting isn't all joy. There's a thread of loneliness woven through it, too.
entry.summary: The essay captures the bittersweet cycle of anticipating spring's cherry blossoms—a recurring moment of beauty that brings both joy and melancholy as we recognize the passage of time and the inevitability of change.

entry.title: Power as a concept.
entry.content:
Money, beauty, talent of whatever kind: these are all forms of power. Most people wish for more of it. If your power grows, you imagine, surely your freedom will increase. Your possibilities will expand. Your happiness will multiply. Your circle of allies will widen.

But if your power grows, it's just as likely that your constraints will increase too. The things you must not do will multiply. So will your misfortunes. So will the rifts among your allies. That much is easy enough to imagine.

Become a person of importance, and you get to ride in a large car fitted with bulletproof glass. But why, exactly, does it need to be bulletproof? Maybe that tells you everything.

Still, it's too easy to just say you don't need power. If you had almost none, would freedom be there waiting for you? Would possibility? Would happiness? Would anyone be at your side? That, too, is hard to say. Maybe yes. Maybe no.

You can study all the history you like, take in as many stories as you want, and the answer to these questions never becomes clear. Period dramas, gangster films, it's all the same. How to handle power is always the central dilemma. "Just the right amount of power" doesn't make for much of a story. And even when a tale is about ordinary life, power is always lurking in the background.

Thanks for visiting Hobonichi again today. The new season of Peaky Blinders is almost here.
entry.summary: The essay explores the paradox that gaining power—whether through money, beauty, or talent—simultaneously increases both freedom and constraints, leaving us unable to definitively answer whether power truly brings happiness or whether powerlessness might offer its own form of liberation.

entry.title: It was 10,000 times, apparently.
entry.content:
Someone at Hobonichi seems to have noticed. Those short pieces I've been writing all this time, hadn't they quietly passed the ten-thousand mark? Well, it's not like I've been counting as I go. Every year when June 6th comes around, I'm aware of how many years it's been. But as for the act of writing every day, I've made that the subject here more than a few times. People are often impressed that I've kept it up so long, which makes it an easy thing to write about. "It must be so hard," they say, or "Aren't there days you just don't want to write?" That kind of reaction is exactly what I was hoping for when I started.

When I launched Hobonichi, I thought the one thing even I could do, if I just committed to it, that would make people happy, was to write every single day without fail. I wanted to be like a shopkeeper who shows up at the store every day. Whether it's good or bad, polished or rough, I can't really judge. In cooking terms, "as long as it's not terrible, that's enough." (I'm stealing that from Yoshiharu Doi.) There's no trick to keeping it going. You decide to do it, and you do it. It truly is something anyone can do if they just do it, but people are kind enough to say, "That's exactly the thing most people can't do."

To keep going every day without a break, well, you try not to get seriously ill, you think of ways to manage when things get desperate. As an extension of simply being alive, it takes a little ingenuity. But as far as effort goes, it feels like a pretty cheap investment.

So, once I heard it might have passed ten thousand, I got curious about when exactly that had been, and I asked ChatGPT. It told me the ten-thousandth one fell on October 21, 2025. And while it was at it, today's installment is apparently number 10,145.

Well, after writing ten thousand of these, pretty much all of my foolishness and failings and sloppiness have been laid bare. That might actually be the best thing to come out of it.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too. Doing what you can do if you just do it turns out to be, well, surprisingly doable.
entry.summary: The author reflects on reaching ten thousand daily essays at Hobonichi, discovering that consistency—simply showing up every day without fail—is achievable for anyone willing to commit, and that this sustained practice of sharing one's authentic, imperfect self is ultimately its own reward.

entry.title: Itoi Shigesato didn't just rave about it — he nearly fainted.
entry.content:
Well, I completely collapsed the other day. First time in a long while. I was so exhausted I felt dizzy, like I couldn't get enough oxygen, and then I just hit a wall.

So where did this happen, you ask? At the Sanseido Kanda Jimbocho Main Store, of all places. The details were covered in yesterday's Hobonichi live text updates, but basically, while Sanseido was still preparing for their opening, the Hobonichi crew was invited in, and each of us got to buy one book. It was a special little event made possible by neighborly ties and various other connections.

I'd already known about Sanseido's plans for the new store, having attended their announcement events and such. But when those plans actually materialize as a real, physical bookstore, it's something else entirely. I have no reason to flatter them, so I'll just say it honestly: there's real lifeblood running through both the concept and the execution. If this were a film, you'd want the credits to list an executive producer, a director, an art director. You can tell that someone thought deeply about it, and then everyone worked together to bring it to life.

It reminds me, actually, of visiting the Ichikawa Zoological and Botanical Garden recently, the one with Punch the monkey. When you're there, you can just feel how the staff have poured their ingenuity and care into creating that place. It's a similar sensation.

The books seem to be telling you something. "Where did I come from, and where do I want to go?" And that feeling, I think, comes from the editorial sensibility behind how the books are gathered and arranged. My eyes, and then my legs, kept being summoned from one book to the next, and sometimes I'd stop and talk with them. Nothing is lined up in some generic, perfunctory way, so you feel compelled to engage with each one properly.

That is why I was so utterly spent. It was like sitting through an endless series of one-on-one meetings with an enormous number of people. Curiosities, hard questions, confessions, temptations, all coming at me without pause. In a way, it felt like being lost in a forest of distractions. (I can't bring myself to call it something dignified like "a forest of knowledge.") I was truly overwhelmed. I should have braced myself better before going in.

So next time, I'm going back as a regular customer, properly prepared for a rematch.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. I made careful note of every book I plan to buy with my own money next time.
entry.summary: The author experiences profound exhaustion upon visiting the newly opened Sanseido bookstore, overwhelmed by the thoughtful curation and intentional arrangement of books that demanded genuine engagement rather than passive browsing. The essay celebrates how spaces created with care and editorial vision can be so mentally and emotionally taxing that they leave us depleted, yet compelled to return.

entry.title: Failure and chance are the ingredients for hope.
entry.content:
I was raised on the principle of "better safe than sorry, and then safer still." I think parents and teachers, regardless of generation, all said something along these lines: "No matter how sure you feel, you never know what might happen. If you consider every possibility and prepare for it, you'll have nothing to worry about, no matter what comes your way." Growing up steeped in that kind of teaching, I think I ended up narrowing my own possibilities considerably.

"Consider every possibility and prepare for it" is certainly one way to avoid failure. In medicine, in rail and aviation, in accounting, it's an essential way of thinking, and genuinely important. But if everyone applies this approach to every situation in life, it only ratchets up the tension, clouds your judgment, and blurs the focus on what truly matters.

It may be late in the game, but what I want to say (to myself as much as anyone) is this: "good judgment" is not the same thing as "considering every possibility" or "being extra, extra careful." (Besides, in this day and age, AI is better at that sort of thing anyway.) What matters far more is never losing sight of what's important, being willing to make a decision even when things aren't perfect, and not putting things off too long. If parents and teachers are going to teach anything, I think it should be things like that.

In the real world, where real people are living and moving, trying a lot of things yields far greater rewards. In that world, failures and chance encounters are precious raw material. Rather than being so cautious you never fail, rather than having a neat logical explanation for everything, the encounters you gain through action, including the failures and the accidents, are worth so much more. I really do think so. Hey, I'm talking to you, me.

The self that was shaped by the adults of the past and the self I am now go back and forth, making our way toward whatever comes next.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. Oh, and yes. "Don't think! Feel!" And "Just do it!"
entry.summary: Excessive caution and risk avoidance, while useful in certain fields, ultimately limit life's possibilities and potential for growth; true wisdom lies in decisive action, embracing failure as valuable experience, and prioritizing what matters over perfect preparation.

entry.title: Musings of a big-sister teacher.
entry.content:
This past week, I went to an elementary school in Akagi, then to Kesennuma, and now I'm back in Tokyo. The last thing I did was visit Tsunakan in Karakuwa to see Ichiyo Kanno. We made a fuss catching up, offered prayers facing the sea, and made sure each other was doing well. We both made each other promise to come to the other's funeral. Lately, that's pretty much all I've been doing. "Don't assume your money or your friends will always be around." And I mean it. The slightest shift in circumstances can change your fate entirely. It's fine to trust that tomorrow will come, but the day after, or ten years from now, or forever? You can't count too much on any of that. You've got to live today. Saying mildly ominous things at just the right moment has always been a specialty of mine, so please forgive me.

Ichiyo said she'd always relied on my boss the way you'd rely on a big brother. But when we met yesterday, she'd been promoted: "Kanako-san is like a father." That's something. My boss, my wife, and now a father figure. Meanwhile, apparently I'm "like a big sister," which means I'm the kind of person she can say anything to. We've clearly transcended all social notions of gender here. On top of that, she sometimes accidentally calls me "sensei," so I've ended up as "Big Sister Sensei." Fine. I'll just go ahead and live the rest of my life as Big Sister Sensei.

In Kesennuma, I saw lots of children. Of course, I pay close attention to children in Tokyo too. As I've gotten older, children have started to look radiant to me. When adults are busy cataloging their various despairs, when they're tallying up the pessimism and the darkness day after day, children are the future simply by being there.

On a related note, AI doesn't actually believe in the future. It doesn't believe. It simply assumes, statistically, that there probably will be one, and works from there. Which, in its own way, I find rather amusing.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. I bumped my ribs on the armrest of a hotel chair, and it kind of hurts.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on the fragility of life and relationships, emphasizing that we must cherish the present moment and the people around us since circumstances can shift unexpectedly. Through encounters with old friends and observations of children, the narrator finds meaning in genuine human connection and the radiance of those who haven't yet learned despair.

entry.title: What do you mean, "feelings of helplessness"?
entry.content:
2026 minus 2011 is, let's see, 15. So it's been 15 years already. Enough time has passed that I find myself doing the subtraction just to confirm it. Yesterday, a friend in Kesennuma said to me, "I thought we'd have to brace ourselves for this being a long battle, like maybe 10 years. But even that's come and gone, and it feels like we got here fast."

In the parking lot of the hotel where we're staying, there are no TV broadcast vans, of course. No crowds either. Walking through town, we remember "that moment, in this place" and say to each other, "Yeah, that's how it was." But this only happens because we're conscious of this special day, March 11th. Probably most of the time, the everyday just flows along here like anywhere else.

I think it's a very good thing that we've almost forgotten we once wished for these ordinary days to return.

For 15 years, I've come to Kesennuma every year on this day. No one has ever asked me, "What will you do this year?" It's always on the schedule. We only discuss how I'll make the trip. I come because I'm meant to see everyone in Kesennuma.

In the first year or two, after coming here, I'd be so depressed on the bullet train ride home. I thought the reason was "a sense of powerlessness." The sadness of "I can't do anything, I haven't done anything." I believed that was the cause of my low spirits. But really, who was I to even claim powerlessness? Looking back now, powerlessness was the starting point. Regardless of whether someone like me is powerless or mighty, here we are 15 years later, with everyone having reclaimed their everyday lives.

That was a "future" built by powerless people, each one of them, pooling their strength together, using their wisdom, using machines and tools, somehow creating it bit by bit. Even my powerlessness was part of those materials. That's how I can think of it now.

This might be the town where I have the most people I want to see. I'm happier than anything to meet everyone in good health.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. Not forgetting. That's the one promise I've kept for 15 years.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on 15 years of annual visits to Kesennuma after the 2011 disaster, realizing that the return of ordinary life—once desperately wished for—is now so complete that forgetting those wishes is itself a blessing, and that even powerlessness, when pooled together, helped rebuild the future.

entry.title: In Kesennuma, now, alone.
entry.content:
I'm in Kesennuma. Already having a wonderful time catching up with people I haven't seen in ages. Saikichi-san's "bappa"—the moment we saw each other, we both said, "You haven't changed a bit, you look so well." If I recall correctly, bappa is about ten years older than me. And after telling me I looked well, she added, "And you look interesting." No one has ever said anything that made me happier. From now on, I'm going to live in a way that makes people say I look interesting. And then there was another older friend, a visitor from Hokkaido, Oda-san from R-tei. I was so glad to see him too. It was the very definition of "what joy it is to have friends visit from afar." I've been influenced by all sorts of people, but what this mentor has done and the things he's told me feel like they've become a model for how I think now (especially lately). When it gets a bit warmer, I'm definitely going to visit him again.

There are certain ways of thinking that I find completely true. Like "haste makes waste," for example. I understand that deeply. But I also completely agree with "strike while the iron is hot." Should I hurry or take the long way around? Even if you tell me to pick one, I have to make a judgment call each time depending on the situation. Talking about all sorts of things, I realize this happens a lot. Sometimes it really is "haste makes waste." Don't panic, don't rush so much. You've failed before by taking shortcuts, haven't you? But, you know, while you're being so calm and collected, you often miss precious opportunities. More often than not, hesitating gets you nowhere. "Just do it now, strike while the iron is hot!" I say this to myself and others all the time. Everyone has experienced both, everyone feels both ways. So many things are truly "both," but we make judgments based on the situation and circumstances. In other words, life is about judgment. Writing about such things in a hotel in Kesennuma before bed.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today. That I've finished writing this—well, that was also a judgment I just made.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on how life requires constant judgment calls between contradictory truths—like "haste makes waste" versus "strike while the iron is hot"—with no single rule applying to every situation.

entry.title: A time machine to the world of 14-year-olds.
entry.content:
Even if I tell you about this film, there's no guarantee everyone will be able to see it. If you have the right connections, or good luck, or chase after it earnestly enough, you'll probably manage to see it. Even now, the theaters showing it are limited, but apparently "screenings" are also possible in places that aren't movie theaters. Still, it's not really a loss if you can't see it. There's nothing particularly rare in the film. It's just about thirty-five ordinary eighth graders living their lives, so in a way, it's like you yourself are already there.

That said, these ordinary eighth graders, ordinary as they are, are all incredibly (to use the trendy way of putting it) "precious."

The title is "Bookmark at 14." It's a documentary film that listens to the stories of thirty-five students in one class at one school and films their daily lives. Each of them has their own story, of course, but the film doesn't deliberately add shading or drama to those stories. All thirty-five are covered more or less equally, so even when you want to know more, you can't follow further. There's just those two months or so of their lives until spring comes and they move up a grade. The camera goes to classrooms, commute routes, and sometimes their homes, but there's a comfortable intimacy in the distance it keeps from these fourteen-year-olds.

For each of these fourteen-year-olds, nothing has really begun yet, and nothing has ended either. Every day, like snow piling up, "now" accumulates upon "now."

Naturally, every viewer will find themselves trying to recall what they were like at fourteen. I too, thinking "I might have been even more childish," got on the time machine and spent about two hours looking around, my eyes darting about the world of fourteen-year-olds. The world these thirty-five ordinary kids showed me was, in the best sense, far more old-fashioned and straightforward than the online world we routinely see now. That's right, I felt reassured. "Living as a person" is actually pretty serious, pretty old-fashioned stuff, isn't it.

I'm grateful to the team behind this film and to these fourteen-year-olds. Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. Apparently, fourteen is the age when we become biologically capable of creating a "family."
entry.summary: This documentary about thirty-five ordinary eighth graders reminds viewers that despite living in a digital age, the experience of being fourteen—suspended between childhood and adulthood, accumulating "now" upon "now"—remains reassuringly old-fashioned and profoundly human.

entry.title: This sentence isn't written on parchment.
entry.content:
What I'm writing here, right now, I'm not writing it "because it's important." I know there are plenty of things far more important than this. There are probably things I ought to say precisely because they're not important. And yet, "setting aside all those things I'm not saying," here I am, writing something or other. Whenever something is written, whenever something is said, there are hundreds of billions of times more things that aren't written, aren't said. So why on earth am I writing this, of all things? If someone were to seriously press me on that, I couldn't give them a proper answer. If there were something I truly wanted to write, I think I'd probably write it, but whether that would actually be something important, I don't know. I think it's okay to say that important things aren't the only things that matter. This might be one of those important things that doesn't look important. It's complicated. I wish I'd started writing about something simpler, but that's too much trouble, so I'll just keep going.

One reason I can write something so apparently wasteful like this is the fact that this medium isn't a "stone tablet" or "parchment" or anything like that. In ancient times, when people wanted to write something, the medium they wrote on was extremely precious. Even "paper," which came much later, was precious. And to get it read by everyone, you had to go through the process of printing and then transporting that paper. Without having to pay those "high costs," we can now say things like "well, well" or "huh" and get away with it. That's the age we're living in. The meaning of precious, the calculation of costs, the weight of what should be written, the qualifications for writing, everything has changed. Once again, we're asking: what's really necessary for writing and communicating? What should be valued? The meaning of learning and knowledge must be changing too. I'd really like to know what form that takes.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi again today. I have a feeling "the richness of what goes unsaid" might be the hint we're looking for.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on how digital media has freed writing from the constraints of precious physical materials, allowing us to communicate trivial thoughts alongside important ones and fundamentally changing what we value in written expression.

entry.title: Dusk is nice, but sunrise is the best.
entry.content:
I went to see those elementary school kids again. The students at Shirakawa Elementary School at the foot of Mount Akagi. Back in November, I'd done a class with them on "How can we live happily even after we become adults?" This time I was invited to participate in a kind of follow-up "presentation event." Those sixth graders I'd talked with are graduating soon. When I'd met them just three months earlier, they'd been worrying about all sorts of things, saying they felt anxious about becoming adults. Now they were holding an event to present what they're thinking about these days. They wanted me to come hear it. Of course I went. I felt like there was some kind of connection pulling me there.

If I tried to write about it in detail I'd never finish, so I'll be very rough about it, but it was wonderful. From guiding me from the school parking lot to the waiting room, the sixth graders handled everything themselves. And get this: every single one of them spoke in a loud, clear voice. Back then, I'd told them that if your voice is too quiet, what you want to communicate won't reach people, and that would be a waste. The kids apparently corrected that right away! The teachers seemed amazed too, but I was astonished.

Each of them spoke about their future selves. Kids who had role models nearby they were learning from. Kids who'd found concrete things they wanted to do and loved. Kids who didn't know what they wanted to become but were enjoying all sorts of things. Everyone presented honestly and happily. They talked about their dreams, but they also included conditions like "I can make money" and "there are days off" and "I can finish at regular hours," which actually felt refreshingly realistic. Best of all, all that anxiety and worry they'd been so eager to talk about before had disappeared for the moment. After they finished presenting, maybe because they felt relieved, every single one of them broke into a smile. I could only think, yes, yes, this is perfect, this is the best. Go, walk, run, fall, rest, slack off, live! Just being energetic right now already earns you full marks. I hope you'll enjoy all those tomorrows beyond tomorrow that I'll never know.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today too. I say this like it's a joke, but I want to invite all of them to my funeral.
entry.summary: A writer returns to an elementary school to see sixth graders present their thoughts about the future, and is moved to find that the anxious children from three months ago are now speaking confidently and honestly about their dreams with both idealism and refreshing realism. The transformation fills the writer with joy and hope for lives they'll never fully witness, creating a connection so profound they jokingly want to invite the students to their funeral.

entry.title: Going through today, too, with "it's no big deal."
entry.content:
There are days when I know I have to get up early the next morning. On those nights, I want to go to bed early. But I have this column, formerly known as "Today's Darling," and I can't sleep until I've finished writing the next day's piece. It would be nice if I could start writing earlier in the day, but when I start early, I end up dawdling and it takes even longer. I often think, maybe I should write a little bit during the day when I have some free time, and then just add to it later. But that's never worked out well. Actually, even when it has worked out, it hasn't made much difference. It doesn't really change anything. Usually I end up throwing away whatever I've started writing. I throw it away without hesitation and write something fresh in the middle of the night, but I can't say that produces anything particularly good either. It's been a little under 28 years now, hasn't it? I've been writing every day, so you'd think I'd have gotten a little better at it, but I haven't.

If this were a "conversation" like the one in Tasogare, which I'm serializing now, when I got to the part about getting "a little better," I'd want to interrupt myself and say, "like Akina Nakamori, you know." And then I'd want someone to come back at me with, "But Nakamori only sang it. The lyrics were written by Etsuko Kisugi." But Shinbo isn't all that familiar with this stuff, so he probably wouldn't say anything like that. He'd just laugh. Nagata has a useless wealth of knowledge about old songs too, but he probably wouldn't touch on pop music. If I really had my way, I'd go on and on about unnecessary things like "just grabbing your sleeve and looking down" or "I wonder if Akina's gotten better at it by now." Going a bit far afield, "Wait, was Iyo still 16?" "No, she was 17, right?" "Well, she's not anymore," and other pointless chatter. But look, I've already written this many words, so that's probably enough, isn't it? I can finish up now, I can go to bed early.

But I really do think, deep down, that I've been getting by all this time on being "nothing special." That I'm truly a guy who's "nothing special." Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. Though I wouldn't want to hear someone else say that about me. Funny, isn't it.
entry.summary: The author reflects on their inability to improve their daily writing routine even after 28 years, ultimately accepting that they've succeeded by being "nothing special"—a self-assessment they're comfortable making themselves but wouldn't want to hear from others.

entry.title: Looking at my own notes.
entry.content:
I have a note that says "a park where a child is playing first." If I organized my thoughts better, maybe I'd phrase it differently, but I haven't tinkered with it at all. I've kept that note just as it is. There's a child, playing first.

There are no words for what the child is doing. Maybe they're skipping. Maybe they're looking around at this and that. If the child's expression seems happy, someone might come join in. Even without knowing what they're doing, you can just say "let's play together" and that's enough. Or maybe you'd consult: "what should we play?" If it were puppies, there'd be no such discussion. They'd just start sniffing each other and chasing around. When one child becomes two children, the playing gets even more fun. Seeing that, another child will probably join in. And one by one, the circle of play expands. The park becomes lively, full of life.

Probably it doesn't even need to be a place called a park. Just a "space" is enough. Because one person is "playing first," there's a beginning. When there's a "space" and there's "someone playing first," there's a "future (the next moment)" right there. I guess I believe in that. I feel like that's all I've been doing.

The play doesn't even have a name yet, but the play is definitely there. The exchange of "let's play" and "play with me" enriches the play itself, enriches the space where playing happens. Spaces for play are born, they age, they disappear, but when someone starts playing there again, they're reborn.

Don't stop the play. Play is a future that keeps beginning, always.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today. Even on days without good news, there are children playing first.
entry.summary: The essay explores how the simple act of one person beginning to play creates a space and invitation for others to join, suggesting that this perpetual cycle of playful initiation is what generates community, possibility, and an ever-renewing future.

entry.title: People of berets, people of wind, people of glasses.
entry.content:
People have been asking me, "Is the reason you've been wearing a beret lately because of the Hobonichi Manga Club?" It's true that manga artists like Osamu Tezuka and Fujiko Fujio A seem to favor berets. I can see why they'd think I was following in their footsteps, but that's not quite it. I've always worn berets from time to time. I used to wear all sorts of hats, not frequently, but I wore them. I got really into those Peaky Blinders style flat caps, rejecting this one and that one, ordering a bunch online. Though honestly, I hardly ever wear those. Anyway, I've always owned several berets, and one day I just thought, "Maybe I'll wear one." Then I started buying even more, wanting to choose between different ones, and before I knew it I was wearing them every day. I decided I'd stick with this until I got tired of it. (But enough about that. Let me tell you about something actually useful.)

How to avoid catching colds and keep your glasses from fogging up! Two birds with one stone, pretty amazing, right? First, for avoiding colds, nothing beats washing your hands. There's that famous question: why don't doctors catch colds even though they examine so many sick people? It's because they wash their hands frequently. So I've been trying to do the same. These days they have those pump dispensers that produce foam, which makes it incredibly easy to wash your hands often. If you don't wear glasses, you can stop reading here. But for those of us who do, listen up. When you wash your hands, wash your glasses with that same foam. While you're washing your hands anyway, use the foam on the lenses and everywhere else on the frames. Then rinse with water, not hot water because that can damage the lenses. Just shake the glasses lightly to get the water off. Any remaining drops can be absorbed with a single tissue. That's all it takes, and your glasses will be cleaner than any amount of wiping could achieve. It's so simple, I think you'll get addicted to it.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi today. I just realized I've been writing the kind of thing you'd find in "Tasogare."
entry.summary: The essay casually explains the author's recent beret-wearing habit before offering practical advice: washing your glasses with hand soap foam when washing your hands is an easy way to keep them clean and prevent fogging while also maintaining good hygiene to avoid colds.

entry.title: Hey peaches and blossoms, let's have a little chat.
entry.content:
Some people probably noticed, but those who forgot don't even realize they've forgotten. Today is March third, that famous "Peach Blossom Festival." It's the main day of Hinamatsuri, when you display hina dolls and eat scattered sushi, hina rice crackers, diamond-shaped mochi, and sweet white sake. Come to think of it, yes, that's right. I have the knowledge, the memories. But surprisingly, everyone just lets it pass by. I suppose it's different for families with little girls or young daughters.

I tried to think about why it's become so forgotten. One reason, I think, is that we don't see TV commercials for hina dolls anymore. Probably hina doll sales aren't big enough to support all those ads like in the old days. For one thing, everyone's homes are smaller now. Once you decide that just the top-tier emperor and empress dolls are enough, you don't need the whole entourage of three court ladies and five musicians. And of course you won't buy all the accompanying furnishings and furniture either. Taking them out and putting them away is such a hassle, after all. It's becoming "quietly kept among family," just like how funerals have shifted to small family services. In other words, it's no longer a festival in everyone's consciousness.

And then there are the peaches. Don't forget, it's the Peach Blossom Festival. But have you actually seen peach blossoms blooming in your neighborhood? Unless you live in a peach-growing region like Yamanashi or Okayama, I don't remember ever being conscious of peach blossoms blooming. I don't really know the difference between plum, peach, and cherry blossoms either, to be honest. I do know they're all part of the rose family, though. In just a little while, the cherry blossoms will start blooming and steal all the attention anyway. Even though everyone pays such attention to peaches when it comes time to eat them.

But this year I remembered. Not that it matters much, but here I am, someone who lives ignoring Valentine's Day and lucky direction sushi rolls, remembering the Peach Blossom Festival and sighing, "I'd forgotten all about it." Peaches, blossoms, I'm sorry, but I have nothing more to say about you. If I remember again next year, I'd like to talk about the same sort of thing.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today. The cherry blossoms will probably be in full bloom again sometime this month, won't they.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on how Hinamatsuri (the Peach Blossom Festival) has faded from public consciousness due to smaller living spaces, declining commercial promotion, and urban disconnect from seasonal nature, even as the author momentarily remembers it. Despite this rare recollection, the author admits having nothing meaningful to add and will likely repeat the same nostalgic lament if remembering it again next year.
